content.text: The Day-1 launch operations plan covers the window from two hours before a Claude feature goes live to the 24-hour mark when elevated monitoring shifts to normal operations. It defines who is online, what they watch, when they check in, how decisions are made, and under what conditions the launch is paused or rolled back. Day-1 operations are not normal production operations — they are a temporary, heightened posture that treats the launch window as an extended incident pre-positioned to execute rapidly.
Day-1 ops ends when the metrics have held at baseline for 24 hours and the on-call team agrees the new version has earned its position in production. It does not end because the clock ran out.

This sub-subtopic covers the pre-launch war room setup, the launch execution sequence, heightened monitoring configuration, the structured check-in cadence, escalation and rollback authority, the T+1h status report, and the Day-1 to Day-2 handoff checklist.

attrs.alt: Day-1 launch timeline from T-2h through T+24h: war room, final gate, launch, heightened monitoring, handoff

content.text: The war room is a shared communication space (Slack channel, video bridge, or both) where all launch stakeholders are present and can coordinate without delay. It is opened two hours before launch and closed after the T+24h handoff.
Owns the launch state; calls Go/No-Go at T-30m; has rollback authority
First responder for API-layer issues; executes rollback if IC calls it
Monitors model-layer metrics (latency, GPU utilization, OOMKill events)
Watches eval score, refusal rate, and safety flag dashboards in real time
Monitors support tickets and Slack for early customer signals
Prepares and sends status updates at T+1h, T+4h, T+24h
Slack channel #launch-[feature-name]-war-room created; all roles pinged and confirmed online
Video bridge open with persistent link shared in channel
Rollback runbook link pinned in channel
Live dashboard links pinned (error rate, latency, eval score, safety flags)
PagerDuty silence windows set for expected metric shifts during ring 0 and ring 1 traffic ramp
Rollback commands staged in a gist/snippet — copy-paste ready, not requiring research under pressure
Customer support briefing delivered; support team knows what the launch changes and what symptoms to escalate
Status page template draft ready for immediate publish if customer-visible degradation occurs
Thirty minutes before launch, the Incident Commander runs a final state check. This is the last opportunity to defer without incurring significant customer communication cost.

#!/bin/bash
# t_minus_30_checklist.sh — automated pre-launch gate

PASS=0
FAIL=0

check() {
  local label="$1"
  local cmd="$2"
  if eval "$cmd" > /dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "  [PASS] $label"
    ((PASS++))
  else
    echo "  [FAIL] $label"
    ((FAIL++))
  fi
}

echo "=== T-30m Final Gate ==="

check "API gateway rollout healthy" \
  "kubectl argo rollouts get rollout claude-gateway -n claude-api | grep -q 'Healthy'"

check "Model serving all pods Running" \
  "kubectl get pods -n claude-serving | grep -v Running | grep -v NAME | wc -l | grep -q '^0$'"

check "Eval score baseline ≥ 98%" \
  "python3 scripts/check_eval_score.py --min-pct 98"

check "Error rate < 0.05% (last 30m)" \
  "python3 scripts/check_error_rate.py --max 0.0005 --window 30m"

check "Rollback runbook accessible" \
  "curl -sf https://wiki/runbooks/api-gateway-rollback | grep -q 'Prerequisites'"

check "On-call roles confirmed (IC, service owner, safety lead)" \
  "python3 scripts/check_war_room_roster.py --required ic,service-owner,safety-lead"

echo ""
echo "=== Result: $PASS passed, $FAIL failed ==="
[ "$FAIL" -eq 0 ] && echo "GO — proceed to launch" || echo "NO-GO — address failures before launch"
exit "$FAIL"

Launch execution for a Claude API feature typically means enabling traffic to the new version — flipping a feature flag, advancing a canary to the first ring, or cutting over a blue-green environment. The exact command depends on the deployment strategy used.

# Option A: Feature flag enable (fastest; most common for Claude feature launches)
python3 scripts/feature_flag_rollback.py --enable claude-feature-v2 --pct 100 \
  --reason "Day-1 launch: feature-v2 GA"

# Option B: Canary first ring (starts at ring0 = internal)
kubectl argo rollouts set image claude-gateway \
  gateway=claude-gateway:v2 -n claude-api
# Canary advances automatically per gate schedule

# Option C: Blue-green promotion
kubectl argo rollouts promote claude-gateway -n claude-api
# Traffic flips from blue to green atomically

# Record launch timestamp (used in post-launch metric comparisons)
LAUNCH_TS=$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)
echo "Launch timestamp: $LAUNCH_TS"
aws ssm put-parameter --name /claude-api/prod/last-launch-ts --value "$LAUNCH_TS" --overwrite
Post in war room: 'Traffic enabled at [timestamp]. Clock started.'
Start the heightened monitoring dashboard on all war room screens
Set a 5-minute Slack reminder for the first check-in
Safety lead: confirm eval score dashboard is receiving data from new version
Service owner: confirm error rate metric shows version='v2' label has appeared

During the first four hours, alert thresholds are tightened and check-in cadence is every 5 minutes. This is not normal production monitoring — it is an attentive posture designed to catch regressions in the first minutes when they are cheapest to roll back.
Catch regressions earlier while blast radius is still small

# tighten_day1_thresholds.py — temporarily lower alert thresholds for Day-1 window
import requests, os

ALERTMANAGER_URL = "http://alertmanager:9093"
PROMETHEUS_RULES_FILE = "infra/alerts/rollout-alerts-day1.yaml"

def activate_day1_thresholds():
    """Replace standard alert rules with Day-1 (tighter) rules."""
    # Load Day-1 rules YAML (pre-authored, checked in)
    with open(PROMETHEUS_RULES_FILE) as f:
        rules_yaml = f.read()
    # Apply via kubectl
    import subprocess
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["kubectl", "apply", "-f", PROMETHEUS_RULES_FILE],
        capture_output=True, text=True
    )
    if result.returncode != 0:
        raise RuntimeError(f"Failed to apply Day-1 thresholds: {result.stderr}")
    print(f"Day-1 alert thresholds active. Revert with: python3 {__file__} --revert")

def revert_to_standard_thresholds():
    import subprocess
    subprocess.run(["kubectl", "apply", "-f", "infra/alerts/rollout-alerts-standard.yaml"])
    print("Standard alert thresholds restored.")

import sys
if "--revert" in sys.argv:
    revert_to_standard_thresholds()
else:
    activate_day1_thresholds()
At T+1h, the Communications Lead posts a structured status report to all stakeholder channels. This is not an incident update — it is a proactive, factual summary of how the launch is going.

On Day-1, the authority to initiate a rollback is simplified relative to normal production: the Incident Commander has unilateral authority to roll back without a committee. The goal is speed, not process.
Automated rollback fires; IC confirms in war room
Verbal call in war room; Service Owner executes immediately; IC confirms in writing
IC calls pause; 15-minute assessment window; IC decides rollback or resume
Safety Lead escalates to IC; IC calls rollback within 5 minutes
IC after Customer Support Lead confirms
IC judgment call; does not require metric threshold

On Day-1, bias strongly toward rollback. The cost of rolling back a good feature is one deployment cycle. The cost of holding a bad feature in production is customer trust, support volume, and potential safety exposure. When in doubt, roll back.

At T+24h, if the launch metrics have held at baseline and no unresolved incidents are open, Day-1 operations conclude and Day-2 steady-state operations begin. The handoff is a deliberate act, not an automatic transition.
All P0/P1 incidents from the launch window are closed or have an owner and resolution timeline
24-hour metrics report confirmed: error rate, P99 latency, eval score all within normal thresholds
Alert thresholds reverted from Day-1 (tightened) to standard production thresholds
War room channel archived; on-call rotation returns to normal schedule
Day-2 operations runbook owner confirmed and briefed on any anomalies observed in the first 24h
Customer-facing status page updated if it showed any degradation during launch window
Handoff Command: Revert Day-1 Alert Thresholds

# Revert to standard (non-tightened) alert thresholds
python3 scripts/tighten_day1_thresholds.py --revert

# Confirm standard rules applied
kubectl get prometheusrule claude-rollout-triggers -n claude-api -o yaml | grep 'for:' | head -5
# Expected: for: 5m (not for: 2m used during Day-1)

# Archive war room channel (Slack API)
curl -X POST https://slack.com/api/conversations.archive \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $SLACK_BOT_TOKEN" \
  -d "channel=$WAR_ROOM_CHANNEL_ID"

# Post handoff notice
echo "Day-1 ops complete. Handoff to Day-2 operations. All metrics stable at T+24h."

The Day-1 launch operations plan is a temporary, heightened posture that runs from T-2h through T+24h. It begins with a war room assembly, a final T-30m gate, and a structured traffic-enable sequence. During the first four hours, alert thresholds are tightened and check-ins occur every 5 minutes. The IC has unilateral rollback authority with a strong bias toward rollback over holding a degraded feature. At T+1h, a structured status report goes to all stakeholder channels. At T+24h, with metrics confirmed stable and all incidents closed, alert thresholds are reverted, the war room is archived, and Day-2 steady-state operations begin.
War room roles: IC (rollback authority), Service Owner, Model Serving Lead, Safety Lead, Comms Lead, Support Lead
T-30m final gate: automated checklist verifies rollout health, error rate, eval score, and war room roster before Go
Day-1 alert thresholds are 50% tighter than standard — designed to catch regressions earlier while traffic is still small
5-minute check-ins via automated Slack report for the first 4 hours; T+1h and T+4h structured status posts to stakeholders
Handoff at T+24h requires explicit confirmation: metrics stable, all incidents closed, thresholds reverted, normal on-call resumed
